coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of polylactic acid bilayer composite films using polyvinyl alcohol based coatings containing functionalized carbon dots and layered clay for active food packaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Long
Wang, Chengyu
Dong, ZhiYe
Yao, Jin
Dong, Fang
Dai, Xianglong
coredata.dc:description: To enhance the inadequate active function and gas barrier properties of fully biodegradable polylactic acid (PLA) film within the active packaging field, the application potential of PLA material was improved by introducing polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) based coatings with excellent active functions. In this work, PVA based active coatings were developed using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) that exhibit antioxidant and antibacterial properties, in conjunction with anthocyanin functionalized layered clay (AF-LDHs) that possess natural barrier properties and secondary active function. Due to the excellent active function of NCDs, original PLA film only coated with NCDs/PVA coatings could also exhibit good active function. Upon the addition of AF-LDHs reaching 3 %, antioxidant activity (ABTS method), antibacterial activity (against Escherichia coli), and UV barrier (280 nm) of PLA bilayer films were enhanced by 76.1 %, 70.3 %, and 53.0 % compared with original PLA film. Antibacterial and antioxidant assays indicated that the incorporation of AF-LDHs at a threshold of 3 % or above could supplement the release of NCDs as a secondary active agent, ensuring a more sustained release of active components. The experiment on preserving the freshness of fresh-cut apples demonstrated that PVA based active coatings can effectively delay the browning process by providing antioxidant and oxygen barrier properties, thereby maintaining the freshness of fresh-cut fruits. These results indicate that combining the active and barrier properties of NCDs and AF-LDHs in PVA based coatings offers great application potential for active food packaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polylactic acid
Polyvinyl alcohol
Active food packaging
Coatings
Carbon dots
Layered clay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of NCDs
Chemical structure of PLA bilayer films
Optical properties of PLA bilayer films
Antioxidant activity of PLA bilayer films
Antibacterial activity of PLA bilayer films
Gas barrier properties of PLA bilayer films
Freshness preservation of PVA based active coatings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images, (b) particle size statistical results, (c) FT-IR spectra, and (d) UV-Vis spectra of NCDs.
FT-IR spectra of PLA bilayer films.
SEM images of surface for (a) PLA, (b) NP-P, (c) LNP-P-1 and (d) LNP-P-5.
SEM images of fracture surface for (a) PLA, (b) NP-P, (c) LNP-P-1 and (d) LNP-P-5.
AFM images of surface for PLA bilayer films (a, e PLA; b, f NP-P; c, g LNP-P-1; d, h, LNP-P-5.).
(a) UV-Vis light transmittance curves and (b) photographic images of PLA bilayer films.
(a) DPPH and (b) ABTS radical scavenging activity of NCDs and PLA bilayer films.
(a) Antibacterial activity against E. coli of NCDs and PLA bilayer films. SEM images of E. coli treated with (b) PLA and (c) NP-P.
(a) Gas barrier properties and (b) water contact angle of PLA bilayer films.
(a) Schematic diagram of fresh-cut apples for freshness preservation, (b) digital photos of fresh-cut apples at room temperature for 3 h. (c) BI, (d)WI, and (e) ΔE* of Blank, NP-P* and LNP-P-3*.
Abbreviation and component of PLA bilayer films.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The mass fraction is based on the total solids content (0.7 g) of PVA based coating.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical and color parameters of PLA bilayer films.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of polylactic acid bilayer composite films using polyvinyl alcohol based coatings containing functionalized carbon dots and layered clay for active food packaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, Xiamen University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Packaging Materials and Technology of Hunan Province, Hunan University of Technology, Zhuzhou 412007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Packaging Materials and Technology of Hunan Province, Hunan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Packaging Materials and Technology of Hunan Province, Hunan University of Technology, Zhuzhou 412007, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To enhance the inadequate active function and gas barrier properties of fully biodegradable polylactic acid (PLA) film within the active packaging field, the application potential of PLA material was improved by introducing polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) based coatings with excellent active functions. In this work, PVA based active coatings were developed using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) that exhibit antioxidant and antibacterial properties, in conjunction with anthocyanin functionalized layered clay (AF-LDHs) that possess natural barrier properties and secondary active function. Due to the excellent active function of NCDs, original PLA film only coated with NCDs/PVA coatings could also exhibit good active function. Upon the addition of AF-LDHs reaching 3 %, antioxidant activity (ABTS method), antibacterial activity (against Escherichia coli), and UV barrier (280 nm) of PLA bilayer films were enhanced by 76.1 %, 70.3 %, and 53.0 % compared with original PLA film. Antibacterial and antioxidant assays indicated that the incorporation of AF-LDHs at a threshold of 3 % or above could supplement the release of NCDs as a secondary active agent, ensuring a more sustained release of active components. The experiment on preserving the freshness of fresh-cut apples demonstrated that PVA based active coatings can effectively delay the browning process by providing antioxidant and oxygen barrier properties, thereby maintaining the freshness of fresh-cut fruits. These results indicate that combining the active and barrier properties of NCDs and AF-LDHs in PVA based coatings offers great application potential for active food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, food preservation and safety techniques in food processing and production have become particularly important worldwide with the continuous improvement of living standards (Riahi et al., 2022). A large amount of food undergoes quality degradation or even spoilage during packaging and storage (Nath et al., 2022). Meanwhile, a large number of non-degradable plastic packaging materials lead to environmental pollution (Song et al., 2022). Therefore, in order to solve the problems in the food packaging industry, eco-friendly active food packaging solutions have become the focus of attention. Typically, these environmentally friendly food packaging consist of biodegradable polymer matrix and active agents that serve active functions (Ordoñez et al., 2022). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), as a degradable polymer with excellent film forming performance, is a material classified as generally recognized as safe (GRAS) by the FDA (Liu et al., 2021). PVA has received a lot of attention in the food packaging industry owing to its good mechanical strength, water solubility, oxygen barrier, chemical stability, transparency, and adhesion (Szabo et al., 2020). However, PVA has some drawbacks, such as a lack of antibacterial and antioxidant activity (Lan et al., 2019). In addition, it is easy to become soft due to moisture absorption in a high humidity environment, which affects the physical protection function of PVA.

According to literature reports, there are generally two methods to improve application of PVA in the active packaging materials (Mao et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2020). Firstly, active agents (plant polyphenol, nanoparticles, and chemosynthesis antibiotics) are introduced to enhance the active functions of PVA films (Singh et al., 2022). Secondly, introducing various active agents into the PVA matrix and fabricating a PVA active coating on traditional polymer films is currently a feasible method (Almasi et al., 2020; Christophliemk et al., 2017). The surface functionalization of traditional packaging films via coating technology can prevent damage of active agents' performance that may occur during direct melting processes, and accelerate the migration of active agents into packaging environment, ultimately effectively exerting the active function of active agents (Almasi et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022). The utilization of biodegradable polylactic acid (PLA) for the coated base film aligns with the principles of the current circular and green economy, contributing to a reduction in the consumption of petroleum resources and the exacerbation of plastic pollution (Dutta and Sit, 2022; Song et al., 2022). Although PLA has been successfully commercialized owing to its good mechanical strength, biocompatibility, and biodegradability under composting conditions. However, original PLA film has certain drawbacks like limited gas barrier properties as well as UV barrier, and poor active functions. These issues can be effectively addressed by applying PVA based active coatings, which do not compromise the inherent characteristics of original PLA film (Apicella et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2018).

In recent years, researchers have employed a large number of active agents to improve active functions and UV barrier properties of PVA matrix (Kumar et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). As a novel type of nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs), has broad application potential in many fields due to their numerous outstanding properties (Wang and Lu, 2022). Based on its excellent optical properties, biological activity, biocompatibility, low toxicity and cost, CDs has been widely used in biomedicine, photoelectric devices and active packaging (Riahi et al., 2022). Various synthetic processes and precursors yield distinct structures and properties, yet they can be considered to possess a core-shell structure. The shell can be readily passivated or functionalized with organic groups, a feature that is especially crucial for the unique characteristics of CDs (Xue et al., 2023). At present, the introduction of different non-metallic elements can enhance certain special functions of CDs. In the field of active packaging, nitrogen functional groups such as amines can be introduced to achieve stronger antibacterial activity (Shaik et al., 2021). Nitrogen doped CDs (NCDs) have attracted the attention of researchers in recent years due to their low toxicity, strong biocompatibility, and good dispersibility (Ezati et al., 2022; Shaik et al., 2021).

To mitigate the potential adverse effects of NCDs on the hydrophilicity and gas permeability of PVA based coatings, as well as to slow the release rate of NCDs as an active agent in PVA matrix, a high aspect ratio anionic layered clay (layered double hydroxides, LDHs) has been introduced to enhance PVA based coatings. By lengthening the diffusion path of gas molecules, gas barrier properties of PVA based coatings significantly can be significantly improved (Mao et al., 2022, 2023). LDHs has received extensive attention in the field of polymer nanocomposites due to its characteristics such as non-toxicity, large specific surface area, and adjustable structure (Qiu et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the incorporation of LDHs nanosheets can also slow the release rate of active agent and extend their efficacy duration, thereby more efficiently preserving food quality. Based on previous researches (Mao et al., 2022, 2023; Wang et al., 2023), plant polyphenols (anthocyanin (ACY), proanthocyanidin, quercetin, etc.) have been used to load on the LDHs nanosheets to realize plant polyphenol functionalized LDHs. As an effective active ingredient of natural plant polyphenols used in the active packaging, ACY with excellent biological activity can been applied to functionalize LDHs in this study (Mao et al., 2023). ACY functionalized LDHs (AF-LDHs) not only extend the release of primary active ingredient but also improve the interfacial interaction between LDHs and PVA matrix. Meanwhile, it can supplement the release of active ingredient in the later stages, acting as a secondary active agent (Almasi et al., 2020).

In this work, NCDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method using glucose and urea. AF-LDHs was prepared through the complexation of ACY with Cu2 + on the LDHs nanosheets. To the best of our knowledge, the application of NCDs and AF-LDHs in PVA based coatings for the fabrication of PLA bilayer films was explored for the first time. The main active functions can be supplied by NCDs, and the barrier properties and secondary active functions were supplied by AF-LDHs. For this, AF-LDHs/NCDs/PVA active coatings of different proportions were coated on the original PLA film to prepare PLA bilayer films, which combine active functions and barrier properties of PVA based coatings, as well as good mechanical strength, transparency and biodegradability of original PLA film. This study investigated chemical structure, morphology, active functions (antibacterial and antioxidant activities), optical, and gas barrier properties of PLA bilayer films, and also explored the practical preservation effect of PLA bilayer films on the fresh-cut apples. All these features indicate that PLA bilayer films may be a viable option for active food packaging application.

Glucose, urea, 2,2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid ammonium salt) (ABTS), and potassium persulfate were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and methanol were bought from Makclin (Shanghai, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) powder (model: EXCEVAL™ AQ-4104) was bought from Kuraray (Japan). EXCEVAL™ AQ-4104 is an ethylene-modified PVA, which maintains steady gas barrier properties in a wide relative humidity range, from dry conditions to 60 % RH (Apicella et al., 2022). Polylactic acid (PLA) films (model: PLA110, 30 μm) were provided by Shenghe (Shandong, China). Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) was obtained through the hydrothermal synthesis technique, which utilized magnesium salt, aluminum salt, and urea as raw materials (Mao et al., 2017). Average size and thickness of LDHs nanosheets were approximately 1500 nm and 40 nm, respectively (Mao et al., 2017, 2018). Anthocyanin functionalized LDHs (AF-LDHs) was prepared through the complexation of ACY with Cu2+ on the LDHs nanosheets (as shown in Fig. S1) (Mao et al., 2023). ACY-Cu2+ coordination compounds form a coating layer of about 4 nm (Mao et al., 2023). Apples were bought from local fresh supermarket (Xiashan, origin: Shandong, China). Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments. The chemicals not indicated above were purchased from a licensed chemical vendor and used immediately after purchase.

Typically, glucose (1 g) and urea (0.5 g) were successively added to DI water (60 mL), which was fully dissolved by ultrasound for 5 min. The reaction solution was then poured into a teflon-lined stainless steel reactor (100 mL) and placed in a drying oven at 160 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the reaction solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged to remove impurities. Subsequently, the reaction solution was filtered (the filter membrane was water-based with a pore size of 0.22 μm). Finally, the filtrate was freeze-dried to obtain NCDs. NCDs should be stored away from light and in a dry environment. The synthetic NCDs was characterized using the methods described in the Supporting Information.

Abbreviation and component of PLA bilayer films are shown in Table 1. Typically, AF-LDHs was uniformly dispersed in DI water (14 mL) after ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. NCDs (0.035 g) was dispersed in AF-LDHs dispersion during the treatment of magnetic stirring for 10 min. Subsequently, PVA powder was dissolved in AF-LDHs dispersion at 95 °C during the treatment of magnetic stirring for 1 h. Finally, 2–3 mL of these coating solutions with different ratios were placed onto original PLA film (corona surface), and spread over by automatic wire bar coater (model: AFA-IV, Tianchen, China), respectively. The coating temperature and speed were set to 50 °C and 50 mm/s respectively. The coating times were set as four times for each film. The coated films were stored at controlled condition (Temperature: 24 °C and RH: 50 %). The characterization method of PLA bilayer films was described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Morphology of NCDs is characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1(a and b). The test results of XPS and fluorescence characteristics for NCDs are analyzed in the Supporting Information (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). From Fig. 1a, it can be seen that NCDs exhibits excellent dispersion in aqueous solutions, presenting a typical spherical shape. By conducting size statistics on these NCDs (Fig. 1b), it reveals that the particle size of most NCDs is between 2.0 and 2.8 nm, indicating that the synthetic NCDs has uniform particle size (∼2.6 nm). Meanwhile, NCDs exhibits obvious lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of ∼0.21 nm in HRTEM image (Fig. 1a inset), corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphene, indicating that there is a graphene like structure inside NCDs (Yang et al., 2021).

Further chemical structure insights of NCDs were obtained through FT-IR and UV-Vis, as shown in Fig. 1(c and d). In the FT-IR spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 1c), there is a wide absorption band caused by stretching vibrations of -OH and N-H appears between 3660 and 3120 cm−1 (Campos et al., 2015; Ezati et al., 2022). The marked orange region shows stretching vibration bands of methyl and methylene. The infrared vibration bands of CC, N-H, C-N, and C-O-C were observed at 1660, 1561, 1406, and 1070 cm−1, respectively (Riahi et al., 2022; Shah et al., 2016). NCDs exhibits completely different infrared absorption characteristics compared with urea and glucose, indicating the formation of new chemical groups under hydrothermal reaction conditions. The comprehensive analysis of microscopic and chemical structure can indicate that NCDs has a graphene structure inside and abundant active groups (-NH2, -OH, etc.) outside (Shaik et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 1d, NCDs exhibit a significant UV absorption near 274 nm, which is attributed to π-π* electronic transition of C–O groups (Wang and Lu, 2022). The obvious UV absorption indicates that NCDs has good UV barrier properties, which plays a decisive role in the UV barrier characteristic of PLA bilayer films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure of PLA bilayer films
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectra of PLA bilayer films are shown in Fig. 2. In the infrared spectrum of original PLA film, the infrared absorption bands at 2994 and 2941 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of C-H group (Cheng et al., 2021). The characteristic infrared absorption band at 1749 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of CO groups. The infrared absorption bands at 1452 and 1384 cm−1 belong to symmetrical and asymmetric deformation vibration absorption of C-H groups, respectively (Cheng et al., 2021). The infrared absorption bands that appear in the orange marked area are mainly related to the stretching vibration of C-O groups (Kim et al., 2019). In addition, the absorption bands at 870 and 754 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of -C-C- and the bending vibration of C-H groups, respectively, which indicates the existence of amorphous and crystalline phase in original PLA film, reflecting the semi crystalline nature of PLA (Patwa et al., 2018). The components in the active coatings (PVA, NCDs and AF-LDHs) do not exhibit corresponding characteristic absorption, which may be due to strong infrared absorption of characteristic groups for PLA.

Surface and fracture surface morphology of PLA bilayer films are shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4, respectively. In Fig. 3a, regular stretching stripes appear on the surface of original PLA film, which may be caused by film processing technology. The surface of NP-P is smoother than that of original PLA film, indicating that PVA matrix has good leveling property (Mao et al., 2022). The surface of LNP-P-1 still maintains good leveling property, and the surface is smooth and flat. In Fig. 3d, a certain amount of protrusions appear on the surface of LNP-P-5. Meanwhile, Fig. 3d (inset) can reveal the partial aggregation of AF-LDHs, indicating that a significant increase in addition of AF-LDHs can significantly increase the roughness of the coating surface.

As shown in Fig. 4a, the thickness of original PLA film reaches approximately 29.8 μm. It can be seen that the internal structure of commercial original PLA film is dense, and there are no visible bubbles and other defects. After adding 0, 1 %, and 5 % AF-LDHs to NCDs/PVA active coatings, an active coating of 4.5–5.1 μm is formed on the original PLA film. All the PVA based active coatings exhibit homogeneous thickness and dense structure, without common defects such as bubbles. Meanwhile, no visible interface defects are found between original PLA film and PVA based active coating, indicating good interfacial compatibility and adhesion of PVA based active coating (Mao et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023). It is noteworthy that the incorporation of AF-LDHs does not markedly alter the leveling property of PVA based coatings nor their interface compatibility with original PLA film. Furthermore, the agglomerates of AF-LDHs also appear in the active coatings of LNP-P-5 in Fig. 4d, which is consistent with the results of surface morphology analysis.

AFM is further used to study the surface roughness of PLA bilayer films, as shown in Fig. 5. It can be seen that the overall surface morphology of PLA, NP-P, and LNP-P-1 is uniform and regular, without distinct structures and irregularities. The root mean square roughness (Rq) of original PLA film is only about 2.119 nm. After coated with NCDs/PVA active coating, Rq of NP-P further decreases to 1.565 nm, due to good film-forming and leveling properties of PVA matrix. When 1 % AF-LDHs is added, Rq of LNP-P-1 increases to a certain extent, but Rq of LNP-P-1 is still lower than that of original PLA film. Rq of LNP-P-5 increases by only 6.9 % compared with original PLA film, reaching 2.265 nm. The clearly visible protrusions in Fig. 5(d and h) are consistent with the protrusion phenomenon observed in Fig. 3d. Therefore, although AF-LDHs improves the surface roughness of PLA bilayer films to a certain extent, the surface of PLA bilayer films is still smooth compared with commercial PLA film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of PLA bilayer films
ce:section.ce:para: UV-Vis light transmittance curves and photographic images of PLA bilayer films are shown in Fig. 6. Related optical and color parameters are presented in Table 2. In Fig. 6a, original PLA film has barely UV absorption due to the inability of UV radiation to reach the ground within UVC range (190–280 nm) absorbed by the ozone layer (Mao et al., 2023). After coated with NCDs/PVA coatings, NP-P exhibits significant UV barrier, with a 39.4 % decrease in UV transmittance at 315 nm compared with original PLA film. The UV barrier properties further increases with addition of AF-LDHs. Compared with original PLA film, the light transmittance of LNP-P-7 decreases by 62.2 % and 17.5 % at 315 and 600 nm, respectively. This reveals that the introduction of NCDs and AF-LDHs exhibits strong UV absorption, which is related to the conjugated structure in NCDs and ACY-Cu2+ Coordination compounds (Koosha and Hamedi, 2019; Travlou et al., 2018). The effect of PLA bilayer films on the transmittance in the visible light region is much less than that in the UV light region, indicating that PVA based active coatings do not obviously cause the loss of visibility and transparency of PLA bilayer films. Based on the calculated opacity results (presented in Table 2), while the opacity value consistently rises with the incorporation of AF-LDHs, it remains relatively low in comparison to transparent films (Chen et al., 2018; Haghighi et al., 2020).

In Fig. 6b, original PLA film maintains excellent transparency and does not affect the text presentation behind the film at all. The color of NP-P significantly deepens after coated with NCDs/PVA active coatings. The color of PLA bilayer films becomes darker with the addition of AF-LDHs, but they still maintain good transparency. Further analysis of the color for PLA bilayer films is conducted using a colorimeter, as show in Table 2. It is found that after coated with NCDs/PVA active coatings, L* and a* decrease, while b* increases significantly. This indicates a obvious tendency to darken, green and yellow. Upon the incorporation of AF-LDHs, L* continues to decrease, while a* and b* increase continuously. Therefore, after the addition of NCDs and AF-LDHs, the brightness of PLA bilayer films decreases and gradually turns brown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of PLA bilayer films
ce:section.ce:para: Antioxidant activity constitutes a crucial attribute of active packaging materials, capable of effectively safeguarding nutrients like proteins and lipids against oxidation and degradation, thereby preserving the flavor and quality of food (Nath et al., 2022). Considering the differences in the assessment of antioxidant activities through various testing methodologies, DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging experiments are used to comprehensively evaluate the antioxidant activity of PLA bilayer films (de Carvalho et al., 2019), as shown in Fig. 7. DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity shows a similar change rule, but it reveals that PLA bilayer films have higher efficiency in scavenging ABTS radicals. This can be attributed to the fact that PVA based active coatings exhibit a higher swelling rate in aqueous ABTS solution than in methanol DPPH solution (Ezati et al., 2022).

From the results of ABTS radical scavenging experiment (Fig. 7b), it can be seen that original PLA film has almost no ABTS radical scavenging ability. After coated with NCDs/PVA coatings, ABTS radical scavenging activity of NP-P reaches 81.9 %, which is due to good antioxidant activity of NCDs. The antioxidant activity of NCDs can be attributed to its surface functional groups that participate in radical scavenging (Ezati et al., 2022; Riahi et al., 2022). The antioxidant activity of PLA bilayer films first decreases and then increases with the addition of AF-LDHs. However, when only 1 % AF-LDHs is added, AF-LDHs in PVA based active coating can exhibit significant barrier properties, prolonging the migration path of NCDs, resulting in a decrease in antioxidant activity to 64.9 %. The antioxidant activity of PLA bilayer films increases with the addition of AF-LDHs (1–7 %). Based on previous research (Mao et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023), there are two competing mechanisms in the release process of active agents. The first aspect involves release of active agents (NCDs and AF-LDHs) in PVA based active coatings. The second aspect is that the incorporation of AF-LDHs can lengthen the migration path of NCDs (main active agent), thereby inhibiting the release of active agents. However, as the incorporation of AF-LDHs (secondary active agent) reaches a certain level (more than 5 %), the total amount of active agents can also increases (more than NP-P). When the addition of AF-LDHs reaches 5 %, the release of active agents in the PVA based active coatings exceeds the barrier effect of AF-LDHs, and ultimately ABTS radical scavenging rate of LNP-P-5 reaches 82.2 %, exceeding antioxidant activity of NP-P (81.9 %). It is worth mentioning that as AF-LDHs increases, the aggregation phenomenon of AF-LDHs may become more severe, leading to a decrease in the final barrier efficiency and an increase in the final release of active agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of PLA bilayer films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8a shows antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli (E. coli) of NCDs and PLA bilayer films. The antibacterial rate of NCDs exceeds 99.95 %, demonstrating excellent antibacterial activity. According to literature reports (Ezati et al., 2022; He et al., 2018; Li et al., 2022; Riahi et al., 2022), there are two types of antibacterial properties of CDs. Firstly, CDs can adsorb onto the cell walls of bacteria by diffusion and electrostatic interactions. This leads to bacterial isolation, blocking nutrient uptake, and interfering with their physiological metabolism. Concurrently, CDs can be capable of penetrating cellular membranes, disrupting the formation of these membranes, and inducing cytoplasmic leakage. Secondly, under the action of light, it can promote the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can cause non-specific damage to bacterial cells and have a bactericidal effect. Original PLA film has little antibacterial activity. After coated with NCDs/PVA active coatings, the antibacterial activity of NP-P reached 77.6 %. The antibacterial activity of LNP-P-1 decreases to 72.1 %, which is due to the decrease in the release rate of NCDs caused by the addition of AF-LDHs. The antibacterial activity of PLA bilayer films improves gradually with the addition of AF-LDHs. The antibacterial activity of LNP-P-7 reaches the maximum value of 88.1 %, showing excellent antibacterial activity. The results reveal that sufficient AF-LDHs not only delays the release of main active agent, but also supplements the total release of active agents as secondary active agent.

The antibacterial action of PVA base active coatings was further confirmed through micromorphology of E. coli treated with PLA and NP-P, as shown in Fig. 8(b and c). From Fig. 8b, it can be seen that E. coli treated with original PLA film exhibits a typical rod-shaped morphology, with a complete bacterial structure and a flat surface, indicating that original PLA film does not exhibit antibacterial activity (Ezati et al., 2022; Riahi et al., 2022). In Fig. 8c, after treated with NP-P, a large number of ruptures occurred in E. coli colonies, resulting in incomplete structure of E. coli. Therefore, NCDs can effectively inhibit the proliferation and metabolism of bacteria and play a major antibacterial role in PVA based active coatings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas barrier properties of PLA bilayer films
ce:section.ce:para: Oxygen transmission rate (OTR) and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) are shown in Fig. 9a. OTR of original PLA film is 724 cm3/m2·24 h·0.1 MPa, indicating its high permeability to oxygen. OTR of NP-P decreases to 6.64 cm3/m2·24 h·0.1 MPa, which is 99.1 % lower than that of original PLA film, indicating that PVA can exhibit excellent oxygen barrier properties. OTR of LNP-P-3 decreases by more than 99.8 % in comparison with original PLA film, even compared with NP-P without adding AF-LDHs, it also decreases by 78.3 %. The increase in the amount of AF-LDHs to a certain extent leads to a significant decrease in the oxygen barrier efficiency of PVA based active coatings. Similar to the variation trend of OTR, WVTR of NP-P decreases by 44.1 %. WVTR of LNP-P-7 decreases by 61.2 % and 30.6 % in comparison with original PLA film and NP-P, respectively. Therefore, the gas barrier properties of PLA bilayer films are significantly improved by the incorporation of AF-LDHs. Nevertheless, the barrier ability of PLA bilayer films to oxygen is significantly superior to that of water vapor. This disparity is attributed to the inherent hydrophilicity of PVA matrix, which leads to a greater permeation rate of water vapor through PVA based active coatings compared with the non-polar oxygen molecules.

Fig. 9b shows water contact angle of PLA bilayer films. Water contact angle of original PLA film after corona treatment is 79.27 °, showing a certain degree of hydrophilicity. After coated with NCDs/PVA active coatings, water contact angle of NP-P decreases to the lowest value of 60.87 °, this is closely associated with the hydrophilicity of PVA matrix and NCDs (Lara et al., 2020). Generally speaking, for hydrophilic materials, the larger the surface roughness, the larger the contact area of water droplets, the smaller the water contact angle. Interestingly, Upon the incorporation of AF-LDHs, water contact angle of PLA bilayer films shows a gradually increasing trend. When 7 % AF-LDHs is added, water contact angle increases from 60.87 ° (NP-P) to 71.33 °. This may be due to the physical crosslinking effect between AF-LDHs, NCDs and PVA matrix in the active coatings, which hinders the wetting of water molecules, ultimately leading to an increase in water contact angle (as shown in Fig. S4) (Lei et al., 2021). Therefore, combined with the water contact angle, it can be seen that the incorporation of AF-LDHs can reduce the hydrophilicity of PVA based active coatings, which is helpful for improving the water vapor barrier properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Freshness preservation of PVA based active coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh-cut apples are difficult to preserve due to the cutting process, which renders them susceptible to oxidation and browning upon exposure to air (Preethi et al., 2021). This browning phenomenon is predominantly the result of enzymatic oxidation, specifically the oxidation and polymerization of phenolic compounds catalyzed by polyphenol oxidase (PPO) (Jiang et al., 2021). When cells are damaged and intracellular partitions are disrupted, PPO activity increases, leading to the release and oxidation of phenolic substrates under aerobic conditions, causing browning of fruits and vegetables (Jiang et al., 2021; Preethi et al., 2021). Hence, fresh-cut apples serve as suitable subjects for investigating the efficacy of PVA based active coatings in maintaining freshness. In this experiment, the fresh-cut apple cubes were treated with PVA based active coatings via the impregnation technique, and the changes in the color of fresh-cut apple cubes were recorded at different time, as depicted in Fig. 10.

As illustrated in Fig. 10(a and b), it is evident that the browning degree in fresh-cut apples without PVA based active coatings is markedly greater than in those treated with NP-P* (NCDs/PVA active coatings) and LNP-P-3* (AF-LDHs(3 %)/NCDs/PVA active coatings). Furthermore, a colorimeter is employed to perform color analysis on the fresh-cut apples, providing measurements of browning index (BI), whiteness (WI), and color difference (ΔE*). Even NCDs/PVA active coatings without adding AF-LDHs can significantly decelerate the browning of fresh-cut apples. After adding 3 % AF-LDHs into NCDs/PVA active coatings, compared with the blank sample, BI and ΔE* decreases, and WI increases. This suggests that the browning effect observed in fresh-cut apples treated with LNP-P-3* has been significantly weakened, attributable to its excellent antioxidant and oxygen barrier properties (Khan et al., 2014; Maria Leena et al., 2020). Therefore, PVA based active coatings can exert significant antioxidant and oxygen barrier properties to effectively decelerate the oxidative browning effect of fresh-cut apples.

In this work, active coatings based on PVA were developed by incorporating NCDs and AF-LDHs, and subsequently PLA bilayer films were fabricated by surface coating technology. After surface coating, the surface roughness of PLA bilayer films could significantly decrease and still maintain good transmittance, indicating that PVA based active coatings had excellent leveling and transparency. Due to the excellent active functions of NCDs, only original PLA film coated with NCDs/PVA coating could exhibit good active packaging functions. When the amount of AF-LDHs was only 1 %, the antibacterial and antioxidant activity were significantly reduced, indicating that the presence of AF-LDHs can slow down the release of NCDs as the main active agent. When the addition of AF-LDHs reaches 3 %, antibacterial activity (against E. coli), antioxidant activity (ABTS method), and UV barrier (280 nm) of PLA bilayer films were improved by 70.3 %, 76.1 %, and 53.0 % compared with original PLA film. Antibacterial and antioxidant assays indicated that the incorporation of AF-LDHs at a threshold of 3 % or above could supplement the release of NCDs as a secondary active agent, ensuring a more sustained release of active components. Owing to the inherent superior oxygen barrier of PVA matrix, the oxygen barrier properties experienced a significant enhancement upon application of PVA based coatings onto original PLA film. This enhancement was gradually improved with the incorporation of AF-LDHs. The experiment on preserving the freshness of fresh-cut apples had demonstrated that PVA based active coatings could delay the browning of apples by exerting antioxidant and oxygen barrier properties, achieving the preservation of fresh cut fruits. The results indicated that PLA bilayer films had excellent antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV barrier properties, which had great potential for application in the active food packaging field.

Long Mao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. ZhiYe Dong: Investigation, Formal analysis. Chengyu Wang: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Fang Dong: Investigation, Data curation. Jin Yao: Software, Resources. Xianglong Dai: Visualization, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52103098), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province, China (No. 2021J05265).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2025.120460.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective strategies of sustained release and retention enhancement of essential oils in active food packaging films/coatings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, W., Jiang, H., Rhim, J.W., Cao, J., Jiang, W., 2022, Effective strategies of sustained release and retention enhancement of essential oils in active food packaging films/coatings. Food Chem. 367, 130671. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.130671.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent poly(vinyl alcohol) films containing chlorogenic acid carbon nanodots for food monitoring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, X., Wang, H., Niu, N., Chen, Z., Li, S., Liu, S., Li, J., 2020, Fluorescent poly(vinyl alcohol) films containing chlorogenic acid carbon nanodots for food monitoring. ACS Appl. Nano Mater. 3, 7611-7620. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsanm.0c01229.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development and characterization of novel antimicrobial bilayer films based on polylactic acid (pla)/pickering emulsions
